text: After establishing pneumoperitoneum using an open technique, the surgeon inserts ports sequentially under direct vision. The robotic side cart is docked to the patient's left hip, and the robotic arms are attached to the ports, beginning with the camera arm. The positioning and freedom of movement of the robotic arms are checked.

The surgeon begins the medial to lateral approach by incising the peritoneum and identifying the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The dissection plane is developed close to the back of the IMA, revealing the left ureter and gonadal vessels. The IMA is skeletonized, removing fatty tissue, and Hemologs are applied proximally and distally before dividing the artery. The dissection continues to develop the plane without breaching Toll's fascia.

The surgeon traces the inferior mesenteric vein to the duodenal-jejunal junction, applying hemolog clips before dividing the vein. This action facilitates further development of the medial plane, allowing entry into the lesser sac, which is clearly visualized above the lower border of the pancreas. A swab is placed at the top of the pancreas for easier identification in subsequent steps.

The surgeon releases the colon laterally by connecting it with the previously mobilized medial plane. For splenic flexure mobilization, the R-II robotic arm is disengaged, while R-I with monopolar scissors and R-III with bipolar forceps are used to divide the gastrocolic ligament. The assistant provides downward counter-traction to aid in mobilization. Upon entering the lesser sac, the swab placed over the pancreas is visualized and retrieved. The splenic flexure mobilization is completed by releasing the lateral attachments of the splenocolic ligament.

The surgeon uses articulating monopolar scissors in the right hand to precisely dissect the posterior plane, while providing counter-traction with bipolar forceps in the left hand. The dissection is performed anterior to the presacral fascia to avoid injury to the hypogastric nerves and presacral venous plexus. A peritoneal cut is made two cm above the peritoneal reflection from the right, developing the interior plane. The lateral pillars are dissected, joining up with the anterior plane from the left, while preserving nerve fibers posterior to the seminal vesicles.

The surgeon meticulously develops the anterior plane anterior to the adenovillous fascia, using traction with R-II and counter-traction with R-III. The dissection progresses on both the left and right sides down to the pelvic floor, with careful attention to avoid wide dissection that could tent in nerves.

The surgeon carefully divides the adenovillous fascia anteriorly to thin out the rectal tube, ensuring no diathermy damage occurs. The dissection progresses to reach the pelvic floor, exposing the overlying endopelvic fascia.

The surgeon replaces the right iliac fossa port with a twelve millimeter port to accommodate a roticulating forty-five millimeter laparoscopic stapler. The stapler is used to perform an anterior-posterior transection, with two fire rings ensuring precision. The short length of the stapler facilitates maneuvering within the narrow pelvis. The pelvis is inspected, revealing the intact left and right hypogastric nerves. The smooth, intact total mesorectal excision (TME) package is displayed.

Following the resection of the rectum, the surgeon brings out the spike of the circular stapler at the junction of the staple lines. The anastomosis is performed, and a flexible sigmoidoscopy is used to visually inspect the integrity of the anastomosis.
